hook.text: Before any test, what do you believe? A coin is probably fair. A random person probably doesn't have a rare disease.

buildup.text: The prior is your belief before seeing new evidence. It encodes base rates, experience, and context.

discovery.text: If 1 in 10,000 people have a disease, your prior for any random person is 0.01%. Evidence must be strong to override.

twist.text: Ignoring priors leads to absurd conclusions. A 99%-accurate test gives mostly false positives for rare diseases.

climax.text: The prior is the most controversial part of Bayesian statistics. Critics say it's subjective. Fans say it's honest.

punchline.text: What you believed before the evidence matters.

quiz.question: Why is the prior probability important in Bayesian reasoning?
options: It sets the baseline — without it, even accurate tests give misleading results for rare events
It's only relevant when the data is unreliable
It replaces the need for collecting data
